text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Metastatic Castration-Resistant Prostate Cancer — Disease Reassessment and Goals of Care Discussion

ATTENDING ONCOLOGIST: [Attending Oncologist]
Department of Medical Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 71-year-old Polish-American male with metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer (mCRPC), BRCA2 germline mutant, with progressive disease now involving liver, lungs, bone, and lymph nodes, presenting for urgent follow-up after interval restaging imaging confirmed progression through 2 cycles of carboplatin + cabazitaxel. The primary purpose of today's visit is to review imaging results, reassess performance status and functional trajectory, discuss further treatment options, and engage in a goals-of-care conversation with the patient and family.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski was initially diagnosed with de novo metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer at age 67, with a Gleason 9 (5+4) acinar adenocarcinoma presenting with a PSA of 187 ng/mL, bilateral pelvic lymphadenopathy, and 8 discrete bone metastases involving the L2 vertebra, T9 vertebra, left iliac wing, right acetabulum, right femoral neck, right 4th rib, and bilateral lower ribs. Germline testing revealed a pathogenic BRCA2 p.E1308* variant. Tumor NGS at diagnosis showed concurrent TP53 p.R248Q, PTEN homozygous deletion, RB1 loss, and CDK12 frameshift mutation — a high-risk molecular profile.

He was treated initially with ADT (leuprolide acetate) plus abiraterone/prednisone, achieving an excellent PSA response (nadir 1.1 ng/mL). After approximately 18 months of disease control, he developed biochemical and radiographic CRPC at age 69 with rising PSA to 8.7 ng/mL, new bone lesions, and nodal progression. He was transitioned to enzalutamide 160 mg daily with ADT continuation, again achieving a good PSA response (nadir 1.4 ng/mL) before progressing at age 70.2 years with new bone lesions (total ~17 sites) and enlarging retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy.

Given his germline BRCA2 mutation, he was transitioned to olaparib 300 mg BID per PROfound trial data at age 70.3 years, with initial PSA decline to 4.8 ng/mL at 2 months. He also received palliative EBRT (30 Gy in 10 fractions) to the right proximal femur for pain control around this time. His olaparib course was complicated by cumulative, severe, transfusion-dependent anemia requiring two separate pRBC transfusions at 4 months and 7 months, with progressive thrombocytopenia (nadir 98,000) and neutropenia (ANC nadir 1.4 K/uL), necessitating two dose reductions (300 mg BID → 200 mg BID → 100 mg BID).

At age 71 years, restaging imaging on reduced-dose olaparib identified new hepatic lesions (2 hypodense lesions in right hepatic lobe, 1.1 and 0.9 cm) and indeterminate pulmonary nodules. MRI liver and FDG PET/CT confirmed hepatic metastases (SUVmax 5.8). CT-guided liver biopsy confirmed metastatic prostatic adenocarcinoma, Gleason pattern 5, with focal synaptophysin positivity and a Ki-67 of approximately 60% — raising concern for early treatment-emergent neuroendocrine prostate cancer (t-NEPC). Repeat tumor NGS on the liver biopsy revealed de novo AR amplification (copy number 12), a new AR L702H ligand-binding domain mutation, new PIK3CA E545K, and AURKA amplification — with BRCA2 and TP53 mutations retained. No BRCA2 reversion mutations were identified, suggesting continued PARP inhibitor pathway sensitivity is theoretically intact, but the degree of clinical resistance was clear.

A multidisciplinary tumor board discussion at age 71 years concluded that platinum-based chemotherapy combining carboplatin (AUC 4) and cabazitaxel (20 mg/m2) every 21 days with G-CSF prophylaxis was the most appropriate next-line regimen given BRCA2 mutation, neuroendocrine features, and prior hormonal therapy exhaustion. Olaparib was discontinued. He completed cycle 1 and was hospitalized for febrile neutropenia (ANC nadir 0.3 K/uL, temperature 38.9°C), grade 3 anemia requiring 2 units pRBCs, grade 2 thrombocytopenia (platelets 78,000), and a transient AKI (creatinine peak 2.1 mg/dL). Peripheral neuropathy grade 1 was noted. Doses were reduced: cabazitaxel to 15 mg/m2 and carboplatin to AUC 3 for cycle 2.

He completed 2 cycles total. Today he presents following interval CT chest/abdomen/pelvis performed after cycle 2, which showed clear disease progression: hepatic lesions have grown (now 1.4 and 1.3 cm, plus a new 0.6 cm lesion in segment V), pulmonary nodules have increased in size (right lower lobe 9 mm and 6 mm, up from 5 mm and 4 mm), and the right obturator lymph node has enlarged to 2.4 cm. PSA has risen to 18.6 ng/mL. He continues on ADT (leuprolide acetate).

Today, he presents with his wife. He is clearly more fatigued and functionally limited than at prior visits.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Significant fatigue, described as constant and debilitating, present at rest. Poor appetite and markedly decreased oral intake over the past 4-6 weeks. Reports eating approximately one-third of his usual portions. He has lost approximately 15 lbs over the past 5 months. No fever or chills currently.

Pain: Diffuse bone pain 5/10 on average on current pain regimen (oxycodone ER 20 mg BID with oxycodone IR 5 mg Q4H PRN). Worst pain is in the lumbar spine and left hip, 7/10 at peak. Right hip (previously irradiated) relatively well controlled, 1-2/10.

Gastrointestinal: Anorexia as above. Mild nausea, intermittent. No vomiting. Constipation managed with polyethylene glycol and docusate sodium (opioid-related). No abdominal pain. No jaundice noted by patient; wife reports mild yellow tinge to his eyes over the past week.

Pulmonary: Dyspnea with minimal exertion (walking to the bathroom). No dyspnea at rest currently. No cough. No hemoptysis.

Genitourinary: No hematuria. Urinary output appears decreased per wife's report. Baseline CKD; last creatinine elevated.

Neurological: No new neurological deficits. No bowel or bladder incontinence. No saddle anesthesia. No falls. Baseline cognitive slowing attributed to prior enzalutamide, now stable since discontinuation.

Hematologic: Easy bruising noted on arms. No overt bleeding. History of recurrent transfusion-dependent anemia.

Endocrine/metabolic: Hot flashes (ongoing on ADT, grade 2, stable).

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

General: Mr. Kowalski is a thin, elderly-appearing male, visibly fatigued, in mild distress. He is seated in a wheelchair brought from the car — he states he is too tired to walk long distances in the clinic. He is alert and oriented x3. He is emotional but cooperative. His wife is present throughout the visit.

Vital Signs:
- Blood pressure: 106/68 mmHg (hypotensive compared to baseline; previously hypertensive on amlodipine/lisinopril)
- Heart rate: 98 bpm (mild tachycardia)
- Respiratory rate: 18 breaths/min
- Temperature: 37.0°C
- SpO2: 95% on room air
- Weight: 157 lbs (down from 172 lbs at prior visit approximately 6 weeks ago)
- Height: 5'10"
- ECOG Performance Status: 3 (confined to bed or chair more than 50% of waking hours; limited self-care)

Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Mild scleral icterus bilaterally. Mucous membranes pale and dry. No oral lesions.

Neck: No palpable cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No lower extremity edema today (mild prior edema resolved).

Pulmonary: Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles, wheezes, or rhonchi. Mild tachypnea but comfortable at rest.

Abdomen: Soft, mildly tender in the right upper quadrant on deep palpation. Liver edge palpable approximately 3 cm below the right costal margin, firm. No splenomegaly. No ascites on clinical exam. Mild generalized tenderness of the lower abdominal and pelvic region, consistent with known nodal/bony disease.

Musculoskeletal: Diffuse tenderness over the lumbar spine and left iliac region on palpation. Limited range of motion of left hip due to pain. Right proximal femur without significant tenderness at the radiation site. No new deformity of extremities.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Bilateral lower extremity strength 4/5, symmetric. No focal neurological deficits. No signs of cord compression on exam. Bilateral distal sensory loss (fingertips) — consistent with grade 1 peripheral neuropathy, unchanged from prior.

Skin: Pale. No jaundice visible on exam but scleral icterus present as noted. No skin lesions or rashes. Old EBRT field on right thigh/hip shows mild residual hyperpigmentation, well healed.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (TODAY)

Complete Blood Count:
- Hemoglobin: 7.1 g/dL (critically low; down from 8.8 g/dL post-transfusion at last visit)
- Hematocrit: 21.4%
- MCV: 94 fL (normocytic)
- Platelets: 88,000/uL (thrombocytopenia, grade 2, down from 102,000)
- ANC: 1.2 K/uL (grade 2 neutropenia)
- WBC: 2.4 K/uL

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 133 mEq/L (mild hyponatremia)
- Potassium: 3.9 mEq/L
- Bicarbonate: 22 mEq/L
- Creatinine: 1.9 mg/dL (eGFR estimated 36 mL/min; worsening from baseline 1.4 mg/dL)
- BUN: 34 mg/dL
- Total bilirubin: 1.8 mg/dL (elevated, up from 0.9 mg/dL; new hepatic compromise)
- Direct bilirubin: 1.2 mg/dL
- ALT: 68 U/L (mildly elevated)
- AST: 91 U/L (elevated)
- Alkaline phosphatase (ALP): 418 U/L (significantly elevated, up from 287 U/L; hepatic and/or osseous disease progression)
- Albumin: 2.7 g/dL (hypoalbuminemia, reflecting malnutrition and hepatic compromise)
- Total protein: 5.8 g/dL
- Glucose: 138 mg/dL (mildly elevated; T2DM, on metformin — metformin held today given AKI risk)

Tumor Markers:
- PSA: 18.6 ng/mL (rising; up from 11.8 ng/mL at time of initiating carboplatin/cabazitaxel and up from 14.3 ng/mL at end of olaparib)
- LDH: 689 U/L (markedly elevated; indicates high disease burden and/or hepatic involvement)

Coagulation:
- PT/INR: 1.3 (borderline; consistent with early hepatic synthetic dysfunction)
- PTT: 34 seconds

Iron studies: Ferritin 680 ng/mL (elevated, consistent with systemic inflammation/disease burden).

Testosterone: 24 ng/dL (confirmed castrate)

---

IMAGING RESULTS

CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis (most recent, reviewed today):
The liver now contains at least 3 lesions in the right hepatic lobe: the two previously identified lesions have grown to 1.4 cm and 1.3 cm from 1.2 cm and 1.0 cm on the prior study, and a new 0.6 cm lesion has appeared in segment V. This is unambiguous progression of hepatic metastatic disease through 2 cycles of chemotherapy. The lung nodules in the right lower lobe have grown — the dominant nodule is now 9 mm (up from 5 mm), and the second nodule is 6 mm (up from 4 mm). Given this growth pattern and the clinical context, these are very likely pulmonary metastases rather than incidental findings. The right obturator lymph node has enlarged to 2.4 cm. Bone windows on CT suggest stable or possibly progressive osseous disease, though a dedicated bone scan was not repeated at this time.

Prior PET/CT (from age 71, reviewed for reference): Confirmed hepatic metastases with SUVmax 5.8. Extensive osseous hypermetabolic disease. Pulmonary nodules low-avidity at that time.

---

ASSESSMENT

Mr. Kowalski is a 71-year-old retired steelworker with a 4-year history of high-risk metastatic prostate cancer who has now progressed through every available standard line of systemic therapy: ADT + abiraterone (first-line, mCSPC), enzalutamide (second-line, mCRPC), olaparib (third-line, mCRPC, BRCA2-directed), and now carboplatin + cabazitaxel (fourth-line, 2 cycles only). He has clear imaging and biochemical progression through his most recent regimen, with visceral metastases now involving the liver and likely lungs, extensive bone metastases, and nodal disease. He has the additional burden of transfusion-dependent anemia, worsening renal function (eGFR ~36), hepatic compromise as evidenced by rising bilirubin, transaminases, and ALP with hypoalbuminemia — all pointing to rapidly declining organ reserve. His performance status has declined to ECOG 3.

Key prognostic indicators are deeply concerning:
- ECOG PS 3 (significant functional limitation)
- Hemoglobin 7.1 g/dL (transfusion-dependent anemia, third transfusion in 2 months)
- Albumin 2.7 g/dL (severe hypoalbuminemia)
- Total bilirubin 1.8 mg/dL with direct hyperbilirubinemia (hepatic metastasis effect)
- ALP 418 U/L, LDH 689 U/L (high disease burden markers)
- PSA doubling trajectory (from 11.8 to 18.6 ng/mL over approximately 6 weeks)
- Progressive visceral disease (liver, lung) — a well-established poor prognostic feature in mCRPC
- Weight loss of 15 lbs over 5 months
- Worsening renal function (eGFR ~36)

Further systemic chemotherapy options are extremely limited and likely to cause more harm than benefit given his current functional status and organ compromise. Considerations reviewed:

1. Lutetium-177 PSMA-617 (Pluvicto): Theoretically relevant given PSMA expression seen on prior imaging. However, current hepatic dysfunction (bilirubin 1.8 mg/dL, ALP 418 U/L) and ECOG PS 3 are relative-to-absolute contraindications per the VISION trial eligibility criteria and standard prescribing considerations. Salivary gland and renal toxicity risk is further compounded by existing CKD (eGFR ~36). I do not believe this is a safe or appropriate option in his current state.

2. Docetaxel: Not previously administered (deferred at diagnosis per patient preference due to CKD); however, the combination of ECOG 3, thrombocytopenia, renal and hepatic compromise, and prior severe toxicity from cabazitaxel essentially precludes safe use. Docetaxel hepatic metabolism via CYP3A4 is a concern with bilirubinemia.

3. Mitoxantrone: Palliative option rarely used in modern mCRPC management; bone marrow reserve is inadequate given current cytopenias, and the clinical benefit would be modest at best.

4. Clinical trials: Clinical trial options were reviewed comprehensively. The patient does not meet eligibility criteria for any currently open trials at our institution or at nearby academic centers, primarily due to ECOG PS exclusions (most trials require PS 0-2) and end-organ compromise.

5. Best supportive care and hospice: Given the totality of his clinical situation — exhaustion of standard therapy options, ECOG PS 3 with trajectory toward PS 4, visceral progression, significant comorbidity burden, and the high likelihood that further chemotherapy will worsen rather than improve his quality of life — this is now the most appropriate path forward.

I spent considerable time reviewing the imaging with the patient and his wife, explaining that the scan showed the liver spots have grown and new ones have appeared despite the chemotherapy, and that the lung spots are very likely cancer as well. I was direct but compassionate in communicating that the cancer is behaving aggressively and is no longer responding to any of the treatments we have available, and that pursuing further chemotherapy carries high risks of serious harm — hospitalization, infection, organ failure — with very low probability of meaningful benefit at this point.

I also affirmed the excellent care he has received and his remarkable resilience through multiple treatment lines. I emphasized that stopping further cancer-directed treatment is not "giving up" but rather a shift toward ensuring the time he has is comfortable and spent in the setting he chooses.

---

PLAN

1. Chemotherapy discontinuation: Carboplatin + cabazitaxel is discontinued after 2 cycles. No further disease-directed systemic chemotherapy will be pursued at this time.

2. ADT continuation: Leuprolide acetate will be continued. While the cancer has progressed through hormone therapy, discontinuing ADT at this stage risks a testosterone flare and can exacerbate bone pain. The patient and family expressed no objection to continuing the injections, which are well tolerated.

3. Palliative care and hospice engagement:
   - Palliative care team has been involved; formal hospice evaluation is being initiated today.
   - Patient and wife verbally agreed to home hospice enrollment, which will be arranged through our social work team.
   - Goals of care documented: Comfort-focused. DNR/DNI (advanced directive on file). Durable power of attorney: wife.
   - Home hospice agency to coordinate ongoing medication management, symptom monitoring, nursing visits, and social support.

4. Pain management:
   - Current regimen: Oxycodone ER 20 mg BID + oxycodone IR 5 mg Q4H PRN (approximately 3-4 breakthrough doses/day per patient report).
   - Given level of pain and trajectory, transitioning to transdermal fentanyl 25 mcg/hour patch (replaced every 72 hours) for basal analgesia. Oral oxycodone IR 5 mg Q4H PRN retained as breakthrough until fentanyl reaches steady state.
   - Bowel regimen intensified: senna 2 tabs BID + polyethylene glycol daily.
   - Pain service follow-up arranged; hospice nurse will monitor and can titrate per standing orders.

5. Anemia management:
   - Hemoglobin 7.1 g/dL — symptomatic (dyspnea, fatigue). Patient will receive 2 units pRBCs today in the infusion center (third transfusion this episode).
   - In the hospice setting, transfusions can be continued on a case-by-case basis for symptom palliation per patient goals, though frequency may increase as hepatic disease progresses.
   - Metformin is held indefinitely given renal and hepatic compromise; endocrinology/PCP to be notified.

6. Zoledronic acid: Discontinued. The benefit/burden ratio of IV bone-directed therapy is unfavorable in the hospice setting. Adequate pain management takes priority.

7. Dyspnea management: Low-dose oral morphine (per hospice protocol) for dyspnea as needed. SpO2 95% at rest — supplemental oxygen at home may be prescribed by hospice.

8. Nutritional support: Appetite stimulant (dexamethasone low-dose) discussed but patient declined further steroids. Nutritional supplements (high-calorie oral supplements) recommended; hospice dietitian to assist. Goals are comfort-oriented — no tube feeding or parenteral nutrition.

9. Nausea: Ondansetron 4-8 mg PO Q8H PRN continued.

10. Medications held: Metformin (held indefinitely given CKD, hepatic compromise). Lisinopril (held given hypotension, BP 106/68; discuss with hospice regarding further antihypertensive management — likely not needed). Amlodipine — held for same reason.

11. Follow-up: No further oncology follow-up appointments scheduled at this time. Patient will be followed exclusively by hospice team going forward. I have provided my direct contact information to the family for any urgent questions. I will communicate today's plan and the patient's current clinical status to his primary care physician and to the hospice medical director.

I have documented this extensively. I want to acknowledge that this was an exceptionally difficult conversation — Mr. Kowalski has fought this disease with great courage over the past 4 years. He expressed that he has had a good life and that he is grateful for the time treatment has given him. He wants to be home with his wife, his children, and grandchildren. That is what we will help him achieve.

Total visit time: 65 minutes. Greater than 50% of time spent in goals-of-care counseling and care coordination.

[Attending Oncologist signature block]
Department of Medical Oncology
